nativeRange.habitat: Shallow tropical reefs of the Indo-Pacific, from the Red Sea and East African coast across to the Great Barrier Reef and southern Japan. Tends to attach to rockwork in lagoonal and reef-flat environments, often in modest light and gentle flow.

decorPreferences: live rock with crevices for the foot to anchor
stable rockwork the anemone can attach to
moderate flow without direct powerhead blast
covered powerhead intakes to prevent the anemone being drawn in if it wanders

diet.notes: Largely self-sufficient via symbiotic zooxanthellae, but has been reported to respond well to occasional spot-feeding of small meaty marine foods — mysis, finely chopped shrimp, or small pieces of marine fish — roughly once per week. Generally advised to keep feedings small; this species is an enthusiastic eater and overfeeding has been linked to fission stress and bailing.

goodWith: established reef fish that learn to avoid the anemone (most tangs, larger wrasses, dwarf angels)
most LPS, SPS, and soft corals placed beyond the anemone's contact range
avoidWith: small or slow-moving fish (gobies, dragonets, small blennies) — have been reported to be stung and consumed
shrimp other than known commensals (ornamental shrimp may be caught)
other anemones or corals placed within tentacle contact range
soft-bodied invertebrates that may wander onto the oral disc

imageCandidates.notes: In-tank reef shot, 900x675. Sharp, in-focus oral disc showing the diagnostic short bead-like carpet tentacles with a pink-centered mouth against vibrant reef surroundings — best represents the hobbyist look. Recommended.
Susan Prior, 1500x1500. Sharp, complete animal in-situ including the column/pedal foot, pink oral disc and dense beaded tan-pink carpet tentacles. Whole-subject framing.
Susan Prior, 1024x1024. Sharp top-down view of a complete specimen with green-tan radial beaded carpet disc; adds colour/angle variety.

summary: A small carpet anemone (typically 5–15 cm) widely traded for nano and mixed reefs. Available in a huge range of color morphs. Reproduces readily by fission. Generally considered easy to keep, but small fish that contact the disc have been reported to be stung and eaten.
careNotes:
The Mini Maxi Carpet Anemone (Stichodactyla tapetum) is a widely available trade anemone, prized for its small adult size, brilliant color morphs, and ease of care in nano and mid-sized reef tanks. It is a distinct species from the much larger S. gigantea (True Carpet) and S. haddoni (Haddon's Carpet) — both of which are advanced-care, fish-eating giants. S. tapetum stays small (most captive specimens are 5–10 cm across the disc, with larger individuals reaching about 15 cm) and is generally considered beginner-friendly.

Key care points:

• Placement: Unlike its larger cousins, this species prefers to attach its foot into rockwork rather than burying in sand. A sand bed is not required. Provide a stable rock with crevices and let the anemone choose its own spot — moving it after attachment can damage the pedal disc.

• Lighting: Generally advised in the 100–250 PAR range. Sources differ — some hobby guides report success under modest light (~100 PAR), others advise stronger (200+ PAR). Tolerant of a wide range provided acclimation is gradual.

• Flow: Moderate, indirect flow is generally advised. Direct powerhead blast tends to cause the anemone to retract and may trigger wandering.

• Temperament and fish safety: Despite its small size, this is still a carpet anemone with a potent sting. Multiple hobbyist reports document small fish — gobies, dragonets, juvenile fish — being stung on contact and consumed. Larger, faster, or experienced reef fish typically learn to avoid the anemone and coexist without issue. Treat it as semi-aggressive: safe with most appropriately sized reef fish, dangerous to slow or small bottom-dwellers.

• Reef compatibility: Generally considered reef-safe. It does not wander as aggressively as larger carpets once settled, but corals placed within direct tentacle range can be stung. Allow a few centimeters of clearance.

• Clownfish hosting: Although it is a carpet anemone, S. tapetum does NOT reliably host clownfish. Clownfish typically ignore it or are rejected by it. Buyers wanting a clownfish-hosting anemone should choose Entacmaea quadricolor (Bubble Tip), Heteractis magnifica, or one of the larger Stichodactyla species instead.

• Color morphs: Available in an extraordinary range of colors — rainbow, ultra red, ultra orange, green, blue, pink-centered, metallic, and many hybrids. All color forms share the same care profile.

• Propagation: Reproduces readily in captivity by binary fission (longitudinal splitting), particularly when stressed by parameter changes, lighting shifts, or handling. A single specimen has been reported to become multiple animals over a year. Some keepers deliberately stress specimens to induce fission, but unforced splitting in stable conditions is also common.

• Handling: Wear gloves. The nematocysts are potent enough to cause skin irritation on contact with thin skin, and stings to the eyes or mouth have been reported in handling accidents.

• Acclimation: Drip-acclimate over 1–2 hours. Newly introduced specimens may bail (detach and drift) before settling — keep powerhead intakes screened.
breedingNotes: Sexual reproduction (broadcast spawning of eggs and sperm) has been reported in the wild but is not practiced in the hobby. The dominant captive-propagation mode is asexual binary fission — the anemone longitudinally splits into two genetically identical clones. Fission has been reported to occur spontaneously in stable systems and can be induced by transient stressors (parameter swings, brief handling). Fission propagation is well-established in the hobby and is understood to account for much of the captive-bred trade supply, including the wide variety of color morphs available today.

primary.name: Reef Chasers — Mini-Maxi Carpet Anemone Care Guide
primary.notes: Used as primary after the assigned LiveAquaria URL returned 404. Provided temperature, pH, dKH, calcium, lighting (PAR), flow, care level, diet/feeding, temperament, reef-safe, clownfish-hosting note, and size.

additional.name: TankFacts — Mini Carpet Anemone (Stichodactyla tapetum)
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters: temperature 76–80°F (24–27°C), pH 7.8–8.4, salinity 1.023–1.026, dKH 8–12, calcium 380–450 ppm, magnesium 1200–1400 ppm. Confirmed native range from the Red Sea and East Africa across the Indo-Pacific to Japan and the Great Barrier Reef. Confirmed the species does not reliably host clownfish.

additional.name: AquaInfo — Stichodactyla tapetum (Mini Carpet Anemone)
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (family Stichodactylidae), adult size (6–10 cm disc diameter, occasional 20 cm), minimum tank ~50 L for one specimen, and asexual reproduction by fission. Temperature 24–25°C and salinity 1.023–1.025 confirm overlap with the other sources. Size spread across all sources (~4 cm hobbyist reports to ~20 cm wild) led me to set adultSizeCm to 5–15 cm as a fair midrange of captive specimens.

additional.name: Reef Addicts — Invert of the Week: the Mini Carpet Anemone
additional.notes: Hobbyist field account used to corroborate fish-safety concerns (documented goby stung and consumed), strong potency of nematocysts despite small size, fission propagation (one specimen became four), and absence of reliable clownfish hosting. Drives the semi-aggressive temperament rating despite the 'peaceful' label some retailers use.
